name: Ceva's & Menelaus's Theorems
whatIsIt: Two twin theorems about ratios along a triangle's sides. CEVA'S THEOREM tests CONCURRENCY: three cevians AD, BE, CF (each from a vertex to the opposite side) meet at ONE point exactly when (BD/DC)·(CE/EA)·(AF/FB) = 1. MENELAUS'S THEOREM tests COLLINEARITY: a straight line crossing the three sides gives the SAME product = 1 (in magnitude). Same product, opposite jobs — one for a point, one for a line.
whyNeeded: Concurrency (do three lines meet?) and collinearity (do three points lie on a line?) are constant Olympiad questions. Ceva and Menelaus answer both with one ratio product instead of heavy coordinate work.
realLifeUses: Proving the medians of a triangle meet at the centroid
Showing three points lie on one straight line
Trusses and frameworks where supports must meet at a point
Any design needing three lines to be concurrent

vocabulary.meaning: A segment from a triangle's vertex to a point on the opposite side.
Three or more lines passing through one common point.
Three or more points lying on one straight line.

story.title: Do They Meet?
story.text: Neha draws triangle ABC with three cevians and asks, 'Do they meet at one point?' Sir says, 'Ceva! Multiply the three side-ratios: (BD/DC)·(CE/EA)·(AF/FB). If it equals 1, they're concurrent.' For the medians each ratio is 1, so 1·1·1 = 1 — they meet (at the centroid!). 'And if a straight LINE cuts the sides, the same product is 1 — that's Menelaus.' Neha smiles: 'One product answers both.'
story.extractedProblem: For the three medians, what is (BD/DC)·(CE/EA)·(AF/FB)?
story.answerInStory: 1 — each median hits a midpoint so every ratio is 1, and 1·1·1 = 1, proving the medians are concurrent.

visual.caption: Ceva tests whether three cevians meet at one point; Menelaus tests whether three points lie on a line. Both hinge on the same ratio product equalling 1.

props.label: A line cutting the sides: product = 1 (Menelaus)

standardMethod.summary: Walk around the triangle forming the ratios BD/DC, CE/EA, AF/FB. For three cevians, set the product = 1 to test/force concurrency (Ceva). For a straight line crossing the sides, the same product = 1 tests collinearity (Menelaus). Solve for any unknown ratio.
steps: Label the division points and form BD/DC, CE/EA, AF/FB in order.
Ceva: three cevians are concurrent ⟺ the product = 1.
Menelaus: three points are collinear ⟺ the product = 1 (in magnitude).
Plug in the known ratios and solve for the missing one.

workedExamples.problem: For the three medians of a triangle, find (BD/DC)·(CE/EA)·(AF/FB).
steps: Each median meets a midpoint, so every ratio = 1.

workedExamples.problem: Cevians are concurrent with BD/DC = 1/2 and CE/EA = 1/3. Find AF/FB.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using Ceva for collinear points or Menelaus for concurrent lines.
commonMistakes.fix: Ceva = three cevians through one POINT (concurrency). Menelaus = three points on one LINE (collinearity).

commonMistakes.mistake: Forming the ratios in a jumbled order.
commonMistakes.fix: Go around the triangle consistently: BD/DC, then CE/EA, then AF/FB.

teachBackPrompt: Draw a triangle and its three medians. Show a grown-up that each ratio is 1, so (BD/DC)·(CE/EA)·(AF/FB) = 1 — proving by Ceva that the medians meet at one point (the centroid). Then explain how Menelaus uses the same product for three points on a line.

revisionCard.summary: Ceva: three cevians concurrent ⟺ (BD/DC)(CE/EA)(AF/FB) = 1. Menelaus: three points collinear ⟺ same product = 1 (magnitude). Ceva = point, Menelaus = line. Form the ratios in order around the triangle.

rememberIt.hook: Ratio product = 1: Ceva for a point, Menelaus for a line

studentQuestions.q: How do I remember which is which?
studentQuestions.a: Ceva has a 'v' like a Vertex meeting-point (concurrency); Menelaus's line 'lays' flat like a straight line (collinearity).

studentQuestions.q: Why do the medians always concur?
studentQuestions.a: Each median goes to a midpoint, so all three ratios are 1 and the product is 1 — Ceva then guarantees they meet, at the centroid.
